[[File:Takeda mon.svg|20px]] [[Takeda clan]] | unit = | Rank = [[Daimyo]] | commands = [[:en:Shinpu Castle|Shinpu Castle]] | battles = {{Plainlist| * [[:en:Siege of Kanbara|Siege of Kanbara]] * [[:en:Siege of Futamata|Siege of Futamata]] * [[:en:Battle of Mikatagahara|Battle of Mikatagahara]] * [[:en:Siege of Takatenjin (1574)|Siege of Takatenjin]] * [[:en:Siege of Yoshida Castle|Siege of Yoshida]] * [[:en:Battle of Nagashino|Battle of Nagashino]] * [[:en:Battle of Omosu|Battle of Omosu]] * [[:en:Battle of Tenmokuzan|Battle of Tenmokuzan]]{{KIA}} }} }} {{nihongo|'''Takeda Katsuyori'''|武田 勝頼||extra=1546 – 3 April 1582}} was a Japanese ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' (military lord) of the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], who was famed as the head of the [[Takeda clan]] and the successor to the legendary warlord [[:en:Takeda Shingen|Takeda Shingen]]. He was son-in-law of [[:en:Hojo Ujiyasu|Hojo Ujiyasu]], ''daimyō'' of Hojo clan. ==Early life== He was the son of [[:en:Takeda Shingen|Shingen]] by the daughter of [[:en:Suwa Yorishige (daimyo)|Suwa Yorishige]] ([[:en:Posthumous name|Posthumous name]]:{{nihongo|''[[Suwa-goryōnin]]''|諏訪御料人||extra=real name, Koihime}}).{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=978-1-59020-730-7 |page=209 |author-link=Hiroaki Sato (translator)}} Katsuyori's children included Takeda Nobukatsu and Katsuchika.{{Cite web|url=http://www2.harimaya.com/sengoku/html/takeda.html|title=兵庫県西部、播磨地方の情報を中心に日本の旅の想い出を発信しています|language=Japanese|website=www2.harimaya.com}} Katsuyori, first known as {{nihongo|'''Suwa Shirō Katsuyori'''|諏訪四郎勝頼}}, succeeded to his mother's [[Suwa clan]] and gained Takatō Castle as the seat of his domain. After his elder brother [[:en:Takeda Yoshinobu|Takeda Yoshinobu]] died, Katsuyori's son Nobukatsu became heir to the Takeda clan, making Katsuyori the ''de facto'' ruler of the Takeda clan.{{cite book |last1=Turnbull |first1=Stephen |title=Battles of the Samurai |date=1987 |publisher=Arms and Armour Press |isbn=978-0-85368-826-6 |location=London |pages=79–94}} Takeda Katsuyori built [[:en:Shinpu Castle|Shinpu Castle]], a new and larger castle at Nirasaki and transferred his residence there in 1581. ==Military life== In 1569, Katsuyori defeated [[Hojo Ujinobu]] at [[:en:Siege of Kanbara|Siege of Kanbara]]{{cite web|url=https://www.jalan.net/kankou/spt_22382af2170020709/ |title=蒲原城|publisher=じゃらん|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1572, Katsuyori successfully took a [[Tokugawa clan]] possession in the [[:en:Siege of Futamata|Siege of Futamata]], and participated in the [[:en:Battle of Mikatagahara|Battle of Mikatagahara]] against the Oda-Tokugawa alliance.{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1-85409-523-4 |page=219,222–223,230}} In 1573, Katsuyori took charge of the Takeda family after the death of Shingen and fought the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In 1574, he [[:en:Siege of Takatenjin (1574)|captured Takatenjin castle]], which even his father had not managed to do. This gained him the support of other members of the Takeda clan. In 1575, he suffered a terrible loss at the [[:en:Battle of Nagashino|Battle of Nagashino]], defeated by one of the earliest recorded uses of [[:en:Volley fire (infantry tactic)|volley fire]] (by [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]'s 3,000 muskets), and losing a large part of his forces as well as a number of Takeda's generals and retainer.{{cite book |last1=Turnbull |first1=Stephen |title=The Samurai |date=1977 |publisher=Macmillan Publishing Co., Inc. |isbn=978-0-02-620540-5 |location=New York |pages=156–160}} In 1578, Katsuyori incurred the wrath of the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Hōjō family]] by helping [[:en:Uesugi Kagekatsu|Uesugi Kagekatsu]] against [[:en:Uesugi Kagetora|Uesugi Kagetora]] who was [[:en:Hōjō Ujiyasu|Hōjō Ujiyasu]]'s seventh son, adopted by and heir to [[:en:Uesugi Kenshin|Uesugi Kenshin]], that initiated the [[:en:Battle of Omosu|Battle of Omosu]] in 1580 against [[:en:Hojo Ujimasa|Hojo Ujimasa]]. In 1581, Katsuyori [[:en:Siege of Takatenjin (1581)|lost Takatenjin fortress]] by [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]], the siege ended with the deaths of 680 men of [[:en:Okabe Motonobu|Okabe Motonobu]] garrison.{{cite book |last1=Turnbull |first1=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |date=2000 |publisher=Cassell & C0 |isbn=1-85409-523-4 |location=London |page=231}} In 1582, Katsuyori [[:en:Siege of Takatō (1582)|lost Takatō castle]] by [[:en:Oda Nobutada|Oda Nobutada]], the only Takeda stronghold in [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano province]] to put up any resistance to Nobunaga's final invasion of Takeda domain, the castle was taken on March the 2nd 1582.{{Cite book |last=Ōta |first=Gyūichi |title=The chronicle of Lord Nobunaga |date=2011 |publisher=Brill |others=J. S. A. Elisonas, Jeroen Pieter Lamers |isbn=978-90-04-20456-0 |location=Leiden |pages=426–441 |oclc=743693801}} ==Death== {{Main|Battle of Tenmokuzan}} After Katsuyori [[:en:Siege of Takatenjin (1581)|lost Takatenjin fortress]] and [[:en:Siege of Takatō (1582)|Takatō castle]], many clans like Kiso and Anayama withdrew their support for Takeda. The Oda-Tokugawa alliance advanced into [[:en:Kai Province|Kai Province]], and laid siege to [[:en:Shinpu Castle|Shinpu Castle]], Katsuyori was unable to hold the castle with his remaining 300-400 men, so he set fire to Shinpu Castle and fled into the Tenmoku mountain. Later, his forces were destroyed by the combined armies of [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at the [[:en:Battle of Tenmokuzan|Battle of Tenmokuzan]], after which Katsuyori, his wife, and his son committed ritual suicide, known as [[:en:Seppuku|Seppuku]].{{rp|231}} It was the end of [[Takeda clan]]. The nun [[:en:Rikei|Rikei]] wrote an account of his wife's suicide and, pitying them, wrote several verses in their honour.{{cite book |last1=Sato |first1=Hiroaki |title=Japanese Women Poets |date=2008 |publisher=M.E. Sharpe, Inc. |isbn=978-0-7656-1784-2 |location=Armonk |pages=137–139}} of Takeda Katsuyori at Tenmokuzan|alt=|center|426x426px]] == Spouse == [[File:Hojo Masako (Takeda Katsuyori's wife).jpg|thumb|Keirin'in (Katsuyori's wife) carrying a [[:en:Naginata|Naginata]] in the [[:en:Battle of Tenmokuzan|Battle of Tenmokuzan]].]] === Toyama Fujin === Takeda Katsuyori married Toyoma Fujin, the adopted daughter of [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]. She died while giving birth to their son Nobukatsu in 1567. === Keirin'in === Katsuyori later married Keirin'in, daughter of [[:en:Hōjō Ujiyasu|Hojo Ujiyasu]]. They had a son and two daughters. In 1582, when Keirin'in was 19, Katsuyori was decisively defeated by Oda Nobunaga, and they had to flee. However, Katsuyori was resigned to die and urged her to leave him. She refused and killed herself (''[[:en:Jigai|Jigai]]''), along with Katsuyori in the [[:en:Battle of Tenmokuzan|Battle of Tenmokuzan]]. Both of his sons died in the battle. ==Family== Father: [[:en:Takeda Shingen|Takeda Shingen]] (1521–1573) Sons: * [[Takeda Nobukatsu]] (1567–1582) * [[Takeda Katsuchika]] (1580–1582) Wives: * [[Toyama Fujin]] * Keirin'in Daughters: * Tei-hime, married Miyahara Yoshihisa * Kougu-hime, married Naitō Tadaoki == References == {{Commons category-inline|Takeda Katsuyori}} {{Reflist}} == Further reading == * {{cite book | title=Japanese women poets: an anthology |author=Hiroaki Sato | year=2008 | publisher=M.E. Sharpe, Inc }} *[http://www.interq.or.jp/pure/miyatate/takeda-katsuyori.htm Takeda Katsuyori no Saiki (in Japanese)] *[https://web.archive.org/web/20070614061236/http://www.city.kofu.yamanashi.jp/kanko/history/0103.htm Yamanashi Prefecture page on Takeda Katsuyori (in Japanese)] *Shibatsuji Shunroku 柴辻俊六 and Hirayama Masaru 平山優. ''Takeda Katsuyori no Subete'' 武田勝頼のすべて. Tokyo: Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha 新人物往来社, 2007. *Shibatsuji Shunroku 柴辻俊六, ''Takeda Katsuyori'' 武田勝頼. Tokyo: Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha 新人物往来社, 2003. {{openhistory}} {{People of the Sengoku period|state=autocollapse}} ]] {{Suwa Faith}} [[file:Katsuyori died at Mt.Tenmoku 02.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Ukiyoe|Ukiyoe]] [[en:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[cs:Kacujori Takeda]] [[de:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[eo:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[es:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[fa:تاکدا کاتسویوری]] [[fr:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[id:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[it:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[ja:武田勝頼]] [[ko:다케다 가쓰요리]] [[ms:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[nl:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[pl:Katsuyori Takeda]] [[pt:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[ro:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[ru:Такэда Кацуёри]] [[sr:Такеда Кацујори]] [[th:ทาเกดะ คัตสึโยริ]] [[tr:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[uk:Такеда Кацуйорі]] [[zh:武田勝賴]] [[zh-min-nan:Takeda Katuyori]] {{wikidata link|Q662482}} [[Category:1582 deaths]] [[Category:Daimyo]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Suicides by seppuku]] [[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:Takeda clan]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

{{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|WD=Q6639853}}; {{ill|Takeda Nobuyuki|WD=Q11124800}}; {{ill|Takeda Yoshinobu|WD=Q837273}}; {{ill|Ōbai-in|WD=Q6639644}}|family={{ill|Takeda clan|WD=Q1210114}}|native_name=武田勝頼|resting_place={{ill|Keitokuin|WD=Q11514665}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q662482]] Wikidata name: Takeda Katsuyori [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese warlord (1546–1582)}} {{Use dmy dates|date=March 2022}} {{Infobox officeholder | name = Takeda Katsuyori
武田 勝頼 | image = File:Takeda Katsuyori.jpg | office = Head of [[Takeda clan]] | term_start = 1573 | term_end = 1582 | predecessor = [[:en:Takeda Shingen|Takeda Shingen]] | successor = | spouse = Toyama Fujin

[[File:Takeda mon.svg|20px]] [[Takeda clan]] | unit = | Rank = [[Daimyo]] | commands = [[:en:Shinpu Castle|Shinpu Castle]] | battles = {{Plainlist| * [[:en:Siege of Kanbara|Siege of Kanbara]] * [[:en:Siege of Futamata|Siege of Futamata]] * [[:en:Battle of Mikatagahara|Battle of Mikatagahara]] * [[:en:Siege of Takatenjin (1574)|Siege of Takatenjin]] * [[:en:Siege of Yoshida Castle|Siege of Yoshida]] * [[:en:Battle of Nagashino|Battle of Nagashino]] * [[:en:Battle of Omosu|Battle of Omosu]] * [[:en:Battle of Tenmokuzan|Battle of Tenmokuzan]]{{KIA}} }} }} {{nihongo|'''Takeda Katsuyori'''|武田 勝頼||extra=1546 – 3 April 1582}} was a Japanese ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' (military lord) of the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], who was famed as the head of the [[Takeda clan]] and the successor to the legendary warlord [[:en:Takeda Shingen|Takeda Shingen]]. He was son-in-law of [[:en:Hojo Ujiyasu|Hojo Ujiyasu]], ''daimyō'' of Hojo clan. ==Early life== He was the son of [[:en:Takeda Shingen|Shingen]] by the daughter of [[:en:Suwa Yorishige (daimyo)|Suwa Yorishige]] ([[:en:Posthumous name|Posthumous name]]:{{nihongo|''[[Suwa-goryōnin]]''|諏訪御料人||extra=real name, Koihime}}).{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=978-1-59020-730-7 |page=209 |author-link=Hiroaki Sato (translator)}} Katsuyori's children included Takeda Nobukatsu and Katsuchika.{{Cite web|url=http://www2.harimaya.com/sengoku/html/takeda.html|title=兵庫県西部、播磨地方の情報を中心に日本の旅の想い出を発信しています|language=Japanese|website=www2.harimaya.com}} Katsuyori, first known as {{nihongo|'''Suwa Shirō Katsuyori'''|諏訪四郎勝頼}}, succeeded to his mother's [[Suwa clan]] and gained Takatō Castle as the seat of his domain. After his elder brother [[:en:Takeda Yoshinobu|Takeda Yoshinobu]] died, Katsuyori's son Nobukatsu became heir to the Takeda clan, making Katsuyori the ''de facto'' ruler of the Takeda clan.{{cite book |last1=Turnbull |first1=Stephen |title=Battles of the Samurai |date=1987 |publisher=Arms and Armour Press |isbn=978-0-85368-826-6 |location=London |pages=79–94}} Takeda Katsuyori built [[:en:Shinpu Castle|Shinpu Castle]], a new and larger castle at Nirasaki and transferred his residence there in 1581. ==Military life== In 1569, Katsuyori defeated [[Hojo Ujinobu]] at [[:en:Siege of Kanbara|Siege of Kanbara]]{{cite web|url=https://www.jalan.net/kankou/spt_22382af2170020709/ |title=蒲原城|publisher=じゃらん|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1572, Katsuyori successfully took a [[Tokugawa clan]] possession in the [[:en:Siege of Futamata|Siege of Futamata]], and participated in the [[:en:Battle of Mikatagahara|Battle of Mikatagahara]] against the Oda-Tokugawa alliance.{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1-85409-523-4 |page=219,222–223,230}} In 1573, Katsuyori took charge of the Takeda family after the death of Shingen and fought the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In 1574, he [[:en:Siege of Takatenjin (1574)|captured Takatenjin castle]], which even his father had not managed to do. This gained him the support of other members of the Takeda clan. In 1575, he suffered a terrible loss at the [[:en:Battle of Nagashino|Battle of Nagashino]], defeated by one of the earliest recorded uses of [[:en:Volley fire (infantry tactic)|volley fire]] (by [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]'s 3,000 muskets), and losing a large part of his forces as well as a number of Takeda's generals and retainer.{{cite book |last1=Turnbull |first1=Stephen |title=The Samurai |date=1977 |publisher=Macmillan Publishing Co., Inc. |isbn=978-0-02-620540-5 |location=New York |pages=156–160}} In 1578, Katsuyori incurred the wrath of the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Hōjō family]] by helping [[:en:Uesugi Kagekatsu|Uesugi Kagekatsu]] against [[:en:Uesugi Kagetora|Uesugi Kagetora]] who was [[:en:Hōjō Ujiyasu|Hōjō Ujiyasu]]'s seventh son, adopted by and heir to [[:en:Uesugi Kenshin|Uesugi Kenshin]], that initiated the [[:en:Battle of Omosu|Battle of Omosu]] in 1580 against [[:en:Hojo Ujimasa|Hojo Ujimasa]]. In 1581, Katsuyori [[:en:Siege of Takatenjin (1581)|lost Takatenjin fortress]] by [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]], the siege ended with the deaths of 680 men of [[:en:Okabe Motonobu|Okabe Motonobu]] garrison.{{cite book |last1=Turnbull |first1=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |date=2000 |publisher=Cassell & C0 |isbn=1-85409-523-4 |location=London |page=231}} In 1582, Katsuyori [[:en:Siege of Takatō (1582)|lost Takatō castle]] by [[:en:Oda Nobutada|Oda Nobutada]], the only Takeda stronghold in [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano province]] to put up any resistance to Nobunaga's final invasion of Takeda domain, the castle was taken on March the 2nd 1582.{{Cite book |last=Ōta |first=Gyūichi |title=The chronicle of Lord Nobunaga |date=2011 |publisher=Brill |others=J. S. A. Elisonas, Jeroen Pieter Lamers |isbn=978-90-04-20456-0 |location=Leiden |pages=426–441 |oclc=743693801}} ==Death== {{Main|Battle of Tenmokuzan}} After Katsuyori [[:en:Siege of Takatenjin (1581)|lost Takatenjin fortress]] and [[:en:Siege of Takatō (1582)|Takatō castle]], many clans like Kiso and Anayama withdrew their support for Takeda. The Oda-Tokugawa alliance advanced into [[:en:Kai Province|Kai Province]], and laid siege to [[:en:Shinpu Castle|Shinpu Castle]], Katsuyori was unable to hold the castle with his remaining 300-400 men, so he set fire to Shinpu Castle and fled into the Tenmoku mountain. Later, his forces were destroyed by the combined armies of [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] and [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at the [[:en:Battle of Tenmokuzan|Battle of Tenmokuzan]], after which Katsuyori, his wife, and his son committed ritual suicide, known as [[:en:Seppuku|Seppuku]].{{rp|231}} It was the end of [[Takeda clan]]. The nun [[:en:Rikei|Rikei]] wrote an account of his wife's suicide and, pitying them, wrote several verses in their honour.{{cite book |last1=Sato |first1=Hiroaki |title=Japanese Women Poets |date=2008 |publisher=M.E. Sharpe, Inc. |isbn=978-0-7656-1784-2 |location=Armonk |pages=137–139}} of Takeda Katsuyori at Tenmokuzan|alt=|center|426x426px]] == Spouse == [[File:Hojo Masako (Takeda Katsuyori's wife).jpg|thumb|Keirin'in (Katsuyori's wife) carrying a [[:en:Naginata|Naginata]] in the [[:en:Battle of Tenmokuzan|Battle of Tenmokuzan]].]] === Toyama Fujin === Takeda Katsuyori married Toyoma Fujin, the adopted daughter of [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]. She died while giving birth to their son Nobukatsu in 1567. === Keirin'in === Katsuyori later married Keirin'in, daughter of [[:en:Hōjō Ujiyasu|Hojo Ujiyasu]]. They had a son and two daughters. In 1582, when Keirin'in was 19, Katsuyori was decisively defeated by Oda Nobunaga, and they had to flee. However, Katsuyori was resigned to die and urged her to leave him. She refused and killed herself (''[[:en:Jigai|Jigai]]''), along with Katsuyori in the [[:en:Battle of Tenmokuzan|Battle of Tenmokuzan]]. Both of his sons died in the battle. ==Family== Father: [[:en:Takeda Shingen|Takeda Shingen]] (1521–1573) Sons: * [[Takeda Nobukatsu]] (1567–1582) * [[Takeda Katsuchika]] (1580–1582) Wives: * [[Toyama Fujin]] * Keirin'in Daughters: * Tei-hime, married Miyahara Yoshihisa * Kougu-hime, married Naitō Tadaoki == References == {{Commons category-inline|Takeda Katsuyori}} {{Reflist}} == Further reading == * {{cite book | title=Japanese women poets: an anthology |author=Hiroaki Sato | year=2008 | publisher=M.E. Sharpe, Inc }} *[http://www.interq.or.jp/pure/miyatate/takeda-katsuyori.htm Takeda Katsuyori no Saiki (in Japanese)] *[https://web.archive.org/web/20070614061236/http://www.city.kofu.yamanashi.jp/kanko/history/0103.htm Yamanashi Prefecture page on Takeda Katsuyori (in Japanese)] *Shibatsuji Shunroku 柴辻俊六 and Hirayama Masaru 平山優. ''Takeda Katsuyori no Subete'' 武田勝頼のすべて. Tokyo: Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha 新人物往来社, 2007. *Shibatsuji Shunroku 柴辻俊六, ''Takeda Katsuyori'' 武田勝頼. Tokyo: Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha 新人物往来社, 2003. {{openhistory}} {{People of the Sengoku period|state=autocollapse}} ]] {{Suwa Faith}} [[file:Katsuyori died at Mt.Tenmoku 02.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Ukiyoe|Ukiyoe]] [[en:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[cs:Kacujori Takeda]] [[de:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[eo:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[es:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[fa:تاکدا کاتسویوری]] [[fr:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[id:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[it:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[ja:武田勝頼]] [[ko:다케다 가쓰요리]] [[ms:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[nl:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[pl:Katsuyori Takeda]] [[pt:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[ro:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[ru:Такэда Кацуёри]] [[sr:Такеда Кацујори]] [[th:ทาเกดะ คัตสึโยริ]] [[tr:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[uk:Такеда Кацуйорі]] [[zh:武田勝賴]] [[zh-min-nan:Takeda Katuyori]] {{wikidata link|Q662482}} [[Category:1582 deaths]] [[Category:Daimyo]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Suicides by seppuku]] [[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:Takeda clan]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

He was son-in-law of [[:en:Hojo Ujiyasu|Hojo Ujiyasu]], ''daimyō'' of Hojo clan. ==Early life== He was the son of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Shingen}} by the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige (daimyo)|en|Suwa Yorishige (daimyo)|WD=Q2511179|lt=Suwa Yorishige}} ({{ill|Posthumous name|en|Posthumous name|WD=Q696100|lt=Posthumous name}}:{{nihongo|''[[Suwa-goryōnin]]''|諏訪御料人||extra=real name, Koihime}}).{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=978-1-59020-730-7 |page=209 |author-link=Hiroaki Sato (translator)}} Katsuyori's children included Takeda Nobukatsu and Katsuchika.{{Cite web|url=http://www2.harimaya.com/sengoku/html/takeda.html|title=兵庫県西部、播磨地方の情報を中心に日本の旅の想い出を発信しています|language=Japanese|website=www2.harimaya.com}} Katsuyori, first known as {{nihongo|'''Suwa Shirō Katsuyori'''|諏訪四郎勝頼}}, succeeded to his mother's [[Suwa clan]] and gained Takatō Castle as the seat of his domain. After his elder brother {{ill|Takeda Yoshinobu|en|Takeda Yoshinobu|WD=Q837273|lt=Takeda Yoshinobu}} died, Katsuyori's son Nobukatsu became heir to the Takeda clan, making Katsuyori the ''de facto'' ruler of the Takeda clan.{{cite book |last1=Turnbull |first1=Stephen |title=Battles of the Samurai |date=1987 |publisher=Arms and Armour Press |isbn=978-0-85368-826-6 |location=London |pages=79–94}} Takeda Katsuyori built {{ill|Shinpu Castle|en|Shinpu Castle|WD=Q11502234|lt=Shinpu Castle}}, a new and larger castle at Nirasaki and transferred his residence there in 1581. ==Military life== In 1569, Katsuyori defeated [[Hojo Ujinobu]] at {{ill|Siege of Kanbara|en|Siege of Kanbara|WD=Q2659520|lt=Siege of Kanbara}}{{cite web|url=https://www.jalan.net/kankou/spt_22382af2170020709/ |title=蒲原城|publisher=じゃらん|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1572, Katsuyori successfully took a [[Tokugawa clan]] possession in the {{ill|Siege of Futamata|en|Siege of Futamata|WD=Q1014528|lt=Siege of Futamata}}, and participated in the {{ill|Battle of Mikatagahara|en|Battle of Mikatagahara|WD=Q690464|lt=Battle of Mikatagahara}} against the Oda-Tokugawa alliance.{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1-85409-523-4 |page=219,222–223,230}} In 1573, Katsuyori took charge of the Takeda family after the death of Shingen and fought the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In 1574, he {{ill|Siege of Takatenjin (1574)|en|Siege of Takatenjin (1574)|WD=Q4540826|lt=captured Takatenjin castle}}, which even his father had not managed to do. This gained him the support of other members of the Takeda clan. In 1575, he suffered a terrible loss at the {{ill|Battle of Nagashino|en|Battle of Nagashino|WD=Q428369|lt=Battle of Nagashino}}, defeated by one of the earliest recorded uses of [[:en:Volley fire (infantry tactic)|volley fire]] (by {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}'s 3,000 muskets), and losing a large part of his forces as well as a number of Takeda's generals and retainer.{{cite book |last1=Turnbull |first1=Stephen |title=The Samurai |date=1977 |publisher=Macmillan Publishing Co., Inc. |isbn=978-0-02-620540-5 |location=New York |pages=156–160}} In 1578, Katsuyori incurred the wrath of the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Hōjō family}} by helping {{ill|Uesugi Kagekatsu|en|Uesugi Kagekatsu|WD=Q1376605|lt=Uesugi Kagekatsu}} against {{ill|Uesugi Kagetora|en|Uesugi Kagetora|WD=Q1190934|lt=Uesugi Kagetora}} who was {{ill|Hōjō Ujiyasu|en|Hōjō Ujiyasu|WD=Q943643|lt=Hōjō Ujiyasu}}'s seventh son, adopted by and heir to {{ill|Uesugi Kenshin|en|Uesugi Kenshin|WD=Q311080|lt=Uesugi Kenshin}}, that initiated the {{ill|Battle of Omosu|en|Battle of Omosu|WD=Q610329|lt=Battle of Omosu}} in 1580 against [[:en:Hojo Ujimasa|Hojo Ujimasa]]. In 1581, Katsuyori {{ill|Siege of Takatenjin (1581)|en|Siege of Takatenjin (1581)|WD=Q2275430|lt=lost Takatenjin fortress}} by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, the siege ended with the deaths of 680 men of {{ill|Okabe Motonobu|en|Okabe Motonobu|WD=Q1073455|lt=Okabe Motonobu}} garrison.{{cite book |last1=Turnbull |first1=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |date=2000 |publisher=Cassell & C0 |isbn=1-85409-523-4 |location=London |page=231}} In 1582, Katsuyori {{ill|Siege of Takatō (1582)|en|Siege of Takatō (1582)|WD=Q2901847|lt=lost Takatō castle}} by {{ill|Oda Nobutada|en|Oda Nobutada|WD=Q2446917|lt=Oda Nobutada}}, the only Takeda stronghold in {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano province}} to put up any resistance to Nobunaga's final invasion of Takeda domain, the castle was taken on March the 2nd 1582.{{Cite book |last=Ōta |first=Gyūichi |title=The chronicle of Lord Nobunaga |date=2011 |publisher=Brill |others=J. S. A. Elisonas, Jeroen Pieter Lamers |isbn=978-90-04-20456-0 |location=Leiden |pages=426–441 |oclc=743693801}} ==Death== {{Main|Battle of Tenmokuzan}} After Katsuyori {{ill|Siege of Takatenjin (1581)|en|Siege of Takatenjin (1581)|WD=Q2275430|lt=lost Takatenjin fortress}} and {{ill|Siege of Takatō (1582)|en|Siege of Takatō (1582)|WD=Q2901847|lt=Takatō castle}}, many clans like Kiso and Anayama withdrew their support for Takeda. The Oda-Tokugawa alliance advanced into {{ill|Kai Province|en|Kai Province|WD=Q858076|lt=Kai Province}}, and laid siege to {{ill|Shinpu Castle|en|Shinpu Castle|WD=Q11502234|lt=Shinpu Castle}}, Katsuyori was unable to hold the castle with his remaining 300-400 men, so he set fire to Shinpu Castle and fled into the Tenmoku mountain. Later, his forces were destroyed by the combined armies of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} and {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} at the {{ill|Battle of Tenmokuzan|en|Battle of Tenmokuzan|WD=Q1113509|lt=Battle of Tenmokuzan}}, after which Katsuyori, his wife, and his son committed ritual suicide, known as {{ill|Seppuku|en|Seppuku|WD=Q133462|lt=Seppuku}}.{{rp|231}} It was the end of [[Takeda clan]]. The nun {{ill|Rikei|en|Rikei|WD=Q3432034|lt=Rikei}} wrote an account of his wife's suicide and, pitying them, wrote several verses in their honour.{{cite book |last1=Sato |first1=Hiroaki |title=Japanese Women Poets |date=2008 |publisher=M.E. Sharpe, Inc. |isbn=978-0-7656-1784-2 |location=Armonk |pages=137–139}} of Takeda Katsuyori at Tenmokuzan|alt=|center|426x426px]] == Spouse == [[File:Hojo Masako (Takeda Katsuyori's wife).jpg|thumb|Keirin'in (Katsuyori's wife) carrying a {{ill|Naginata|en|Naginata|WD=Q546116|lt=Naginata}} in the {{ill|Battle of Tenmokuzan|en|Battle of Tenmokuzan|WD=Q1113509|lt=Battle of Tenmokuzan}}.]] === Toyama Fujin === Takeda Katsuyori married Toyoma Fujin, the adopted daughter of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}. She died while giving birth to their son Nobukatsu in 1567. === Keirin'in === Katsuyori later married Keirin'in, daughter of {{ill|Hōjō Ujiyasu|en|Hōjō Ujiyasu|WD=Q943643|lt=Hojo Ujiyasu}}. They had a son and two daughters. In 1582, when Keirin'in was 19, Katsuyori was decisively defeated by Oda Nobunaga, and they had to flee. However, Katsuyori was resigned to die and urged her to leave him. She refused and killed herself (''[[:en:Jigai|Jigai]]''), along with Katsuyori in the {{ill|Battle of Tenmokuzan|en|Battle of Tenmokuzan|WD=Q1113509|lt=Battle of Tenmokuzan}}. Both of his sons died in the battle. ==Family== Father: {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Takeda Shingen}} (1521–1573) Sons: * [[Takeda Nobukatsu]] (1567–1582) * [[Takeda Katsuchika]] (1580–1582) Wives: * [[Toyama Fujin]] * Keirin'in Daughters: * Tei-hime, married Miyahara Yoshihisa * Kougu-hime, married Naitō Tadaoki == References == {{Commons category-inline|Takeda Katsuyori}} {{Reflist}} == Further reading == * {{cite book | title=Japanese women poets: an anthology |author=Hiroaki Sato | year=2008 | publisher=M.E. Sharpe, Inc }} *[http://www.interq.or.jp/pure/miyatate/takeda-katsuyori.htm Takeda Katsuyori no Saiki (in Japanese)] *[https://web.archive.org/web/20070614061236/http://www.city.kofu.yamanashi.jp/kanko/history/0103.htm Yamanashi Prefecture page on Takeda Katsuyori (in Japanese)] *Shibatsuji Shunroku 柴辻俊六 and Hirayama Masaru 平山優. ''Takeda Katsuyori no Subete'' 武田勝頼のすべて. Tokyo: Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha 新人物往来社, 2007. *Shibatsuji Shunroku 柴辻俊六, ''Takeda Katsuyori'' 武田勝頼. Tokyo: Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha 新人物往来社, 2003. {{openhistory}} {{People of the Sengoku period|state=autocollapse}} ]] {{Suwa Faith}} [[file:Katsuyori died at Mt.Tenmoku 02.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Ukiyoe|Ukiyoe]] [[en:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[cs:Kacujori Takeda]] [[de:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[eo:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[es:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[fa:تاکدا کاتسویوری]] [[fr:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[id:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[it:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[ja:武田勝頼]] [[ko:다케다 가쓰요리]] [[ms:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[nl:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[pl:Katsuyori Takeda]] [[pt:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[ro:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[ru:Такэда Кацуёри]] [[sr:Такеда Кацујори]] [[th:ทาเกดะ คัตสึโยริ]] [[tr:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[uk:Такеда Кацуйорі]] [[zh:武田勝賴]] [[zh-min-nan:Takeda Katuyori]] {{wikidata link|Q662482}} [[Category:1582 deaths]] [[Category:Daimyo]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Suicides by seppuku]] [[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:Takeda clan]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

{{ill|Ryusho-in|WD=Q11642024}}|children={{ill|Shūtetsu Daidōji|WD=Q118697860}}; {{ill|Takeda Katsuchika|WD=Q6639650}}; {{ill|Takeda Nobukatsu|WD=Q5369237}}|father={{ill|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404}}|mother={{ill|Suwa Goryōnin|WD=Q8193299}}|relatives={{ill|Katsurayama Nobusada|WD=Q6639678}}; {{ill|Kenshōin|WD=Q6639729}}; {{ill|Kiku-hime|WD=Q6639684}}; {{ill|Nishina Morinobu|WD=Q4168137}}; {{ill|Shinryūin|WD=Q6639775}}; {{ill|Shinshōni|WD=Q10368377}}; {{ill|Takeda Nobuchika|WD=Q6582131}}; {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|WD=Q6639853}}; {{ill|Takeda Nobuyuki|WD=Q11124800}}; {{ill|Takeda Yoshinobu|WD=Q837273}}; {{ill|Ōbai-in|WD=Q6639644}}|family={{ill|Takeda clan|WD=Q1210114}}|native_name=武田勝頼|resting_place={{ill|Keitokuin|WD=Q11514665}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q662482]] Wikidata name: Takeda Katsuyori [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese warlord (1546–1582)}} {{Use dmy dates|date=March 2022}} {{Infobox officeholder | name = Takeda Katsuyori
武田 勝頼 | image = File:Takeda Katsuyori.jpg | office = Head of [[Takeda clan]] | term_start = 1573 | term_end = 1582 | predecessor = {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Takeda Shingen}} | successor = | spouse = Toyama Fujin
Keirin'in | father = {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Takeda Shingen}} | mother = Suwa Goryōnin | children = Takeda Nobukatsu
Takeda Katsuchika
Tei-hime
Kougu-hime | relatives = {{ill|Takeda Yoshinobu|en|Takeda Yoshinobu|WD=Q837273|lt=Takeda Yoshinobu}} (brother)
{{ill|Takeda Nobuchika|en|Takeda Nobuchika|WD=Q6582131|lt=Takeda Nobuchika}} (brother)
{{ill|Nishina Morinobu|en|Nishina Morinobu|WD=Q4168137|lt=Nishina Morinobu}} (brother)
{{ill|Hōjō Ujiyasu|en|Hōjō Ujiyasu|WD=Q943643|lt=Hōjō Ujiyasu}} (father-in-law) | monarch = | birth_date = 1546 | birth_place = {{ill|Kai Province|en|Kai Province|WD=Q858076|lt=Kai Province}} | death_date = {{death date and age|df=yes|1582|4|3|1546}} | death_place = Tenmoku Mountain, {{ill|Kai Province|en|Kai Province|WD=Q858076|lt=Kai Province}} | allegiance = [[Image:Japanese crest Suwa Kajinoha(Black background).svg|20px]] [[Suwa clan]]
[[File:Takeda mon.svg|20px]] [[Takeda clan]] | unit = | Rank = [[Daimyo]] | commands = {{ill|Shinpu Castle|en|Shinpu Castle|WD=Q11502234|lt=Shinpu Castle}} | battles = {{Plainlist| * {{ill|Siege of Kanbara|en|Siege of Kanbara|WD=Q2659520|lt=Siege of Kanbara}} * {{ill|Siege of Futamata|en|Siege of Futamata|WD=Q1014528|lt=Siege of Futamata}} * {{ill|Battle of Mikatagahara|en|Battle of Mikatagahara|WD=Q690464|lt=Battle of Mikatagahara}} * {{ill|Siege of Takatenjin (1574)|en|Siege of Takatenjin (1574)|WD=Q4540826|lt=Siege of Takatenjin}} * {{ill|Siege of Yoshida Castle|en|Siege of Yoshida Castle|WD=Q5055597|lt=Siege of Yoshida}} * {{ill|Battle of Nagashino|en|Battle of Nagashino|WD=Q428369|lt=Battle of Nagashino}} * {{ill|Battle of Omosu|en|Battle of Omosu|WD=Q610329|lt=Battle of Omosu}} * {{ill|Battle of Tenmokuzan|en|Battle of Tenmokuzan|WD=Q1113509|lt=Battle of Tenmokuzan}}{{KIA}} }} }} {{nihongo|'''Takeda Katsuyori'''|武田 勝頼||extra=1546 – 3 April 1582}} was a Japanese ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' (military lord) of the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, who was famed as the head of the [[Takeda clan]] and the successor to the legendary warlord {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Takeda Shingen}}. He was son-in-law of [[:en:Hojo Ujiyasu|Hojo Ujiyasu]], ''daimyō'' of Hojo clan. ==Early life== He was the son of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Shingen}} by the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige (daimyo)|en|Suwa Yorishige (daimyo)|WD=Q2511179|lt=Suwa Yorishige}} ({{ill|Posthumous name|en|Posthumous name|WD=Q696100|lt=Posthumous name}}:{{nihongo|''[[Suwa-goryōnin]]''|諏訪御料人||extra=real name, Koihime}}).{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=978-1-59020-730-7 |page=209 |author-link=Hiroaki Sato (translator)}} Katsuyori's children included Takeda Nobukatsu and Katsuchika.{{Cite web|url=http://www2.harimaya.com/sengoku/html/takeda.html|title=兵庫県西部、播磨地方の情報を中心に日本の旅の想い出を発信しています|language=Japanese|website=www2.harimaya.com}} Katsuyori, first known as {{nihongo|'''Suwa Shirō Katsuyori'''|諏訪四郎勝頼}}, succeeded to his mother's [[Suwa clan]] and gained Takatō Castle as the seat of his domain. After his elder brother {{ill|Takeda Yoshinobu|en|Takeda Yoshinobu|WD=Q837273|lt=Takeda Yoshinobu}} died, Katsuyori's son Nobukatsu became heir to the Takeda clan, making Katsuyori the ''de facto'' ruler of the Takeda clan.{{cite book |last1=Turnbull |first1=Stephen |title=Battles of the Samurai |date=1987 |publisher=Arms and Armour Press |isbn=978-0-85368-826-6 |location=London |pages=79–94}} Takeda Katsuyori built {{ill|Shinpu Castle|en|Shinpu Castle|WD=Q11502234|lt=Shinpu Castle}}, a new and larger castle at Nirasaki and transferred his residence there in 1581. ==Military life== In 1569, Katsuyori defeated [[Hojo Ujinobu]] at {{ill|Siege of Kanbara|en|Siege of Kanbara|WD=Q2659520|lt=Siege of Kanbara}}{{cite web|url=https://www.jalan.net/kankou/spt_22382af2170020709/ |title=蒲原城|publisher=じゃらん|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1572, Katsuyori successfully took a [[Tokugawa clan]] possession in the {{ill|Siege of Futamata|en|Siege of Futamata|WD=Q1014528|lt=Siege of Futamata}}, and participated in the {{ill|Battle of Mikatagahara|en|Battle of Mikatagahara|WD=Q690464|lt=Battle of Mikatagahara}} against the Oda-Tokugawa alliance.{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1-85409-523-4 |page=219,222–223,230}} In 1573, Katsuyori took charge of the Takeda family after the death of Shingen and fought the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In 1574, he {{ill|Siege of Takatenjin (1574)|en|Siege of Takatenjin (1574)|WD=Q4540826|lt=captured Takatenjin castle}}, which even his father had not managed to do. This gained him the support of other members of the Takeda clan. In 1575, he suffered a terrible loss at the {{ill|Battle of Nagashino|en|Battle of Nagashino|WD=Q428369|lt=Battle of Nagashino}}, defeated by one of the earliest recorded uses of [[:en:Volley fire (infantry tactic)|volley fire]] (by {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}'s 3,000 muskets), and losing a large part of his forces as well as a number of Takeda's generals and retainer.{{cite book |last1=Turnbull |first1=Stephen |title=The Samurai |date=1977 |publisher=Macmillan Publishing Co., Inc. |isbn=978-0-02-620540-5 |location=New York |pages=156–160}} In 1578, Katsuyori incurred the wrath of the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Hōjō family}} by helping {{ill|Uesugi Kagekatsu|en|Uesugi Kagekatsu|WD=Q1376605|lt=Uesugi Kagekatsu}} against {{ill|Uesugi Kagetora|en|Uesugi Kagetora|WD=Q1190934|lt=Uesugi Kagetora}} who was {{ill|Hōjō Ujiyasu|en|Hōjō Ujiyasu|WD=Q943643|lt=Hōjō Ujiyasu}}'s seventh son, adopted by and heir to {{ill|Uesugi Kenshin|en|Uesugi Kenshin|WD=Q311080|lt=Uesugi Kenshin}}, that initiated the {{ill|Battle of Omosu|en|Battle of Omosu|WD=Q610329|lt=Battle of Omosu}} in 1580 against [[:en:Hojo Ujimasa|Hojo Ujimasa]]. In 1581, Katsuyori {{ill|Siege of Takatenjin (1581)|en|Siege of Takatenjin (1581)|WD=Q2275430|lt=lost Takatenjin fortress}} by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, the siege ended with the deaths of 680 men of {{ill|Okabe Motonobu|en|Okabe Motonobu|WD=Q1073455|lt=Okabe Motonobu}} garrison.{{cite book |last1=Turnbull |first1=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |date=2000 |publisher=Cassell & C0 |isbn=1-85409-523-4 |location=London |page=231}} In 1582, Katsuyori {{ill|Siege of Takatō (1582)|en|Siege of Takatō (1582)|WD=Q2901847|lt=lost Takatō castle}} by {{ill|Oda Nobutada|en|Oda Nobutada|WD=Q2446917|lt=Oda Nobutada}}, the only Takeda stronghold in {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano province}} to put up any resistance to Nobunaga's final invasion of Takeda domain, the castle was taken on March the 2nd 1582.{{Cite book |last=Ōta |first=Gyūichi |title=The chronicle of Lord Nobunaga |date=2011 |publisher=Brill |others=J. S. A. Elisonas, Jeroen Pieter Lamers |isbn=978-90-04-20456-0 |location=Leiden |pages=426–441 |oclc=743693801}} ==Death== {{Main|Battle of Tenmokuzan}} After Katsuyori {{ill|Siege of Takatenjin (1581)|en|Siege of Takatenjin (1581)|WD=Q2275430|lt=lost Takatenjin fortress}} and {{ill|Siege of Takatō (1582)|en|Siege of Takatō (1582)|WD=Q2901847|lt=Takatō castle}}, many clans like Kiso and Anayama withdrew their support for Takeda. The Oda-Tokugawa alliance advanced into {{ill|Kai Province|en|Kai Province|WD=Q858076|lt=Kai Province}}, and laid siege to {{ill|Shinpu Castle|en|Shinpu Castle|WD=Q11502234|lt=Shinpu Castle}}, Katsuyori was unable to hold the castle with his remaining 300-400 men, so he set fire to Shinpu Castle and fled into the Tenmoku mountain. Later, his forces were destroyed by the combined armies of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} and {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} at the {{ill|Battle of Tenmokuzan|en|Battle of Tenmokuzan|WD=Q1113509|lt=Battle of Tenmokuzan}}, after which Katsuyori, his wife, and his son committed ritual suicide, known as {{ill|Seppuku|en|Seppuku|WD=Q133462|lt=Seppuku}}.{{rp|231}} It was the end of [[Takeda clan]]. The nun {{ill|Rikei|en|Rikei|WD=Q3432034|lt=Rikei}} wrote an account of his wife's suicide and, pitying them, wrote several verses in their honour.{{cite book |last1=Sato |first1=Hiroaki |title=Japanese Women Poets |date=2008 |publisher=M.E. Sharpe, Inc. |isbn=978-0-7656-1784-2 |location=Armonk |pages=137–139}} of Takeda Katsuyori at Tenmokuzan|alt=|center|426x426px]] == Spouse == [[File:Hojo Masako (Takeda Katsuyori's wife).jpg|thumb|Keirin'in (Katsuyori's wife) carrying a {{ill|Naginata|en|Naginata|WD=Q546116|lt=Naginata}} in the {{ill|Battle of Tenmokuzan|en|Battle of Tenmokuzan|WD=Q1113509|lt=Battle of Tenmokuzan}}.]] === Toyama Fujin === Takeda Katsuyori married Toyoma Fujin, the adopted daughter of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}. She died while giving birth to their son Nobukatsu in 1567. === Keirin'in === Katsuyori later married Keirin'in, daughter of {{ill|Hōjō Ujiyasu|en|Hōjō Ujiyasu|WD=Q943643|lt=Hojo Ujiyasu}}. They had a son and two daughters. In 1582, when Keirin'in was 19, Katsuyori was decisively defeated by Oda Nobunaga, and they had to flee. However, Katsuyori was resigned to die and urged her to leave him. She refused and killed herself (''[[:en:Jigai|Jigai]]''), along with Katsuyori in the {{ill|Battle of Tenmokuzan|en|Battle of Tenmokuzan|WD=Q1113509|lt=Battle of Tenmokuzan}}. Both of his sons died in the battle. ==Family== Father: {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Takeda Shingen}} (1521–1573) Sons: * [[Takeda Nobukatsu]] (1567–1582) * [[Takeda Katsuchika]] (1580–1582) Wives: * [[Toyama Fujin]] * Keirin'in Daughters: * Tei-hime, married Miyahara Yoshihisa * Kougu-hime, married Naitō Tadaoki == References == {{Commons category-inline|Takeda Katsuyori}} {{Reflist}} == Further reading == * {{cite book | title=Japanese women poets: an anthology |author=Hiroaki Sato | year=2008 | publisher=M.E. Sharpe, Inc }} *[http://www.interq.or.jp/pure/miyatate/takeda-katsuyori.htm Takeda Katsuyori no Saiki (in Japanese)] *[https://web.archive.org/web/20070614061236/http://www.city.kofu.yamanashi.jp/kanko/history/0103.htm Yamanashi Prefecture page on Takeda Katsuyori (in Japanese)] *Shibatsuji Shunroku 柴辻俊六 and Hirayama Masaru 平山優. ''Takeda Katsuyori no Subete'' 武田勝頼のすべて. Tokyo: Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha 新人物往来社, 2007. *Shibatsuji Shunroku 柴辻俊六, ''Takeda Katsuyori'' 武田勝頼. Tokyo: Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha 新人物往来社, 2003. {{openhistory}} {{People of the Sengoku period|state=autocollapse}} ]] {{Suwa Faith}} [[file:Katsuyori died at Mt.Tenmoku 02.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Ukiyoe|Ukiyoe]] [[en:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[cs:Kacujori Takeda]] [[de:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[eo:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[es:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[fa:تاکدا کاتسویوری]] [[fr:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[id:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[it:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[ja:武田勝頼]] [[ko:다케다 가쓰요리]] [[ms:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[nl:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[pl:Katsuyori Takeda]] [[pt:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[ro:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[ru:Такэда Кацуёри]] [[sr:Такеда Кацујори]] [[th:ทาเกดะ คัตสึโยริ]] [[tr:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[uk:Такеда Кацуйорі]] [[zh:武田勝賴]] [[zh-min-nan:Takeda Katuyori]] {{wikidata link|Q662482}} [[Category:1582 deaths]] [[Category:Daimyo]] [[Category:Suicides by seppuku]] [[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:Takeda clan]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

{{ill|Ryusho-in|WD=Q11642024}}|children={{ill|Shūtetsu Daidōji|WD=Q118697860}}; {{ill|Takeda Katsuchika|WD=Q6639650}}; {{ill|Takeda Nobukatsu|WD=Q5369237}}|father={{ill|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404}}|mother={{ill|Suwa Goryōnin|WD=Q8193299}}|relatives={{ill|Katsurayama Nobusada|WD=Q6639678}}; {{ill|Kenshōin|WD=Q6639729}}; {{ill|Kiku-hime|WD=Q6639684}}; {{ill|Nishina Morinobu|WD=Q4168137}}; {{ill|Shinryūin|WD=Q6639775}}; {{ill|Shinshōni|WD=Q10368377}}; {{ill|Takeda Nobuchika|WD=Q6582131}}; {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|WD=Q6639853}}; {{ill|Takeda Nobuyuki|WD=Q11124800}}; {{ill|Takeda Yoshinobu|WD=Q837273}}; {{ill|Ōbai-in|WD=Q6639644}}|family={{ill|Takeda clan|WD=Q1210114}}|native_name=武田勝頼|resting_place={{ill|Keitokuin|WD=Q11514665}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q662482]] Wikidata name: Takeda Katsuyori [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese warlord (1546–1582)}} {{Use dmy dates|date=March 2022}} {{Infobox officeholder | name = Takeda Katsuyori

[[File:Takeda mon.svg|20px]] [[Takeda clan]] | unit = | Rank = [[Daimyo]] | commands = {{ill|Shinpu Castle|en|Shinpu Castle|WD=Q11502234|lt=Shinpu Castle}} | battles = {{Plainlist| * {{ill|Siege of Kanbara|en|Siege of Kanbara|WD=Q2659520|lt=Siege of Kanbara}} * {{ill|Siege of Futamata|en|Siege of Futamata|WD=Q1014528|lt=Siege of Futamata}} * {{ill|Battle of Mikatagahara|en|Battle of Mikatagahara|WD=Q690464|lt=Battle of Mikatagahara}} * {{ill|Siege of Takatenjin (1574)|en|Siege of Takatenjin (1574)|WD=Q4540826|lt=Siege of Takatenjin}} * {{ill|Siege of Yoshida Castle|en|Siege of Yoshida Castle|WD=Q5055597|lt=Siege of Yoshida}} * {{ill|Battle of Nagashino|en|Battle of Nagashino|WD=Q428369|lt=Battle of Nagashino}} * {{ill|Battle of Omosu|en|Battle of Omosu|WD=Q610329|lt=Battle of Omosu}} * {{ill|Battle of Tenmokuzan|en|Battle of Tenmokuzan|WD=Q1113509|lt=Battle of Tenmokuzan}}{{KIA}} }} }} {{nihongo|'''Takeda Katsuyori'''|武田 勝頼||extra=1546 – 3 April 1582}} was a Japanese ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' (military lord) of the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, who was famed as the head of the [[Takeda clan]] and the successor to the legendary warlord {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Takeda Shingen}}. He was son-in-law of [[:en:Hojo Ujiyasu|Hojo Ujiyasu]], ''daimyō'' of Hojo clan. ==Early life== He was the son of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Shingen}} by the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige (daimyo)|en|Suwa Yorishige (daimyo)|WD=Q2511179|lt=Suwa Yorishige}} ({{ill|Posthumous name|en|Posthumous name|WD=Q696100|lt=Posthumous name}}:{{nihongo|''[[Suwa-goryōnin]]''|諏訪御料人||extra=real name, Koihime}}).{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=978-1-59020-730-7 |page=209 |author-link=Hiroaki Sato (translator)}} Katsuyori's children included Takeda Nobukatsu and Katsuchika.{{Cite web|url=http://www2.harimaya.com/sengoku/html/takeda.html|title=兵庫県西部、播磨地方の情報を中心に日本の旅の想い出を発信しています|language=Japanese|website=www2.harimaya.com}} Katsuyori, first known as {{nihongo|'''Suwa Shirō Katsuyori'''|諏訪四郎勝頼}}, succeeded to his mother's [[Suwa clan]] and gained Takatō Castle as the seat of his domain. After his elder brother {{ill|Takeda Yoshinobu|en|Takeda Yoshinobu|WD=Q837273|lt=Takeda Yoshinobu}} died, Katsuyori's son Nobukatsu became heir to the Takeda clan, making Katsuyori the ''de facto'' ruler of the Takeda clan.{{cite book |last1=Turnbull |first1=Stephen |title=Battles of the Samurai |date=1987 |publisher=Arms and Armour Press |isbn=978-0-85368-826-6 |location=London |pages=79–94}} Takeda Katsuyori built {{ill|Shinpu Castle|en|Shinpu Castle|WD=Q11502234|lt=Shinpu Castle}}, a new and larger castle at Nirasaki and transferred his residence there in 1581. ==Military life== In 1569, Katsuyori defeated [[Hojo Ujinobu]] at {{ill|Siege of Kanbara|en|Siege of Kanbara|WD=Q2659520|lt=Siege of Kanbara}}{{cite web|url=https://www.jalan.net/kankou/spt_22382af2170020709/ |title=蒲原城|publisher=じゃらん|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1572, Katsuyori successfully took a [[Tokugawa clan]] possession in the {{ill|Siege of Futamata|en|Siege of Futamata|WD=Q1014528|lt=Siege of Futamata}}, and participated in the {{ill|Battle of Mikatagahara|en|Battle of Mikatagahara|WD=Q690464|lt=Battle of Mikatagahara}} against the Oda-Tokugawa alliance.{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1-85409-523-4 |page=219,222–223,230}} In 1573, Katsuyori took charge of the Takeda family after the death of Shingen and fought the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In 1574, he {{ill|Siege of Takatenjin (1574)|en|Siege of Takatenjin (1574)|WD=Q4540826|lt=captured Takatenjin castle}}, which even his father had not managed to do. This gained him the support of other members of the Takeda clan. In 1575, he suffered a terrible loss at the {{ill|Battle of Nagashino|en|Battle of Nagashino|WD=Q428369|lt=Battle of Nagashino}}, defeated by one of the earliest recorded uses of [[:en:Volley fire (infantry tactic)|volley fire]] (by {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}'s 3,000 muskets), and losing a large part of his forces as well as a number of Takeda's generals and retainer.{{cite book |last1=Turnbull |first1=Stephen |title=The Samurai |date=1977 |publisher=Macmillan Publishing Co., Inc. |isbn=978-0-02-620540-5 |location=New York |pages=156–160}} In 1578, Katsuyori incurred the wrath of the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Hōjō family}} by helping {{ill|Uesugi Kagekatsu|en|Uesugi Kagekatsu|WD=Q1376605|lt=Uesugi Kagekatsu}} against {{ill|Uesugi Kagetora|en|Uesugi Kagetora|WD=Q1190934|lt=Uesugi Kagetora}} who was {{ill|Hōjō Ujiyasu|en|Hōjō Ujiyasu|WD=Q943643|lt=Hōjō Ujiyasu}}'s seventh son, adopted by and heir to {{ill|Uesugi Kenshin|en|Uesugi Kenshin|WD=Q311080|lt=Uesugi Kenshin}}, that initiated the {{ill|Battle of Omosu|en|Battle of Omosu|WD=Q610329|lt=Battle of Omosu}} in 1580 against [[:en:Hojo Ujimasa|Hojo Ujimasa]]. In 1581, Katsuyori {{ill|Siege of Takatenjin (1581)|en|Siege of Takatenjin (1581)|WD=Q2275430|lt=lost Takatenjin fortress}} by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, the siege ended with the deaths of 680 men of {{ill|Okabe Motonobu|en|Okabe Motonobu|WD=Q1073455|lt=Okabe Motonobu}} garrison.{{cite book |last1=Turnbull |first1=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |date=2000 |publisher=Cassell & C0 |isbn=1-85409-523-4 |location=London |page=231}} In 1582, Katsuyori {{ill|Siege of Takatō (1582)|en|Siege of Takatō (1582)|WD=Q2901847|lt=lost Takatō castle}} by {{ill|Oda Nobutada|en|Oda Nobutada|WD=Q2446917|lt=Oda Nobutada}}, the only Takeda stronghold in {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano province}} to put up any resistance to Nobunaga's final invasion of Takeda domain, the castle was taken on March the 2nd 1582.{{Cite book |last=Ōta |first=Gyūichi |title=The chronicle of Lord Nobunaga |date=2011 |publisher=Brill |others=J. S. A. Elisonas, Jeroen Pieter Lamers |isbn=978-90-04-20456-0 |location=Leiden |pages=426–441 |oclc=743693801}} ==Death== {{Main|Battle of Tenmokuzan}} After Katsuyori {{ill|Siege of Takatenjin (1581)|en|Siege of Takatenjin (1581)|WD=Q2275430|lt=lost Takatenjin fortress}} and {{ill|Siege of Takatō (1582)|en|Siege of Takatō (1582)|WD=Q2901847|lt=Takatō castle}}, many clans like Kiso and Anayama withdrew their support for Takeda. The Oda-Tokugawa alliance advanced into {{ill|Kai Province|en|Kai Province|WD=Q858076|lt=Kai Province}}, and laid siege to {{ill|Shinpu Castle|en|Shinpu Castle|WD=Q11502234|lt=Shinpu Castle}}, Katsuyori was unable to hold the castle with his remaining 300-400 men, so he set fire to Shinpu Castle and fled into the Tenmoku mountain. Later, his forces were destroyed by the combined armies of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} and {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} at the {{ill|Battle of Tenmokuzan|en|Battle of Tenmokuzan|WD=Q1113509|lt=Battle of Tenmokuzan}}, after which Katsuyori, his wife, and his son committed ritual suicide, known as {{ill|Seppuku|en|Seppuku|WD=Q133462|lt=Seppuku}}.{{rp|231}} It was the end of [[Takeda clan]]. The nun {{ill|Rikei|en|Rikei|WD=Q3432034|lt=Rikei}} wrote an account of his wife's suicide and, pitying them, wrote several verses in their honour.{{cite book |last1=Sato |first1=Hiroaki |title=Japanese Women Poets |date=2008 |publisher=M.E. Sharpe, Inc. |isbn=978-0-7656-1784-2 |location=Armonk |pages=137–139}} of Takeda Katsuyori at Tenmokuzan|alt=|center|426x426px]] == Spouse == [[File:Hojo Masako (Takeda Katsuyori's wife).jpg|thumb|Keirin'in (Katsuyori's wife) carrying a {{ill|Naginata|en|Naginata|WD=Q546116|lt=Naginata}} in the {{ill|Battle of Tenmokuzan|en|Battle of Tenmokuzan|WD=Q1113509|lt=Battle of Tenmokuzan}}.]] === Toyama Fujin === Takeda Katsuyori married Toyoma Fujin, the adopted daughter of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}. She died while giving birth to their son Nobukatsu in 1567. === Keirin'in === Katsuyori later married Keirin'in, daughter of {{ill|Hōjō Ujiyasu|en|Hōjō Ujiyasu|WD=Q943643|lt=Hojo Ujiyasu}}. They had a son and two daughters. In 1582, when Keirin'in was 19, Katsuyori was decisively defeated by Oda Nobunaga, and they had to flee. However, Katsuyori was resigned to die and urged her to leave him. She refused and killed herself (''[[:en:Jigai|Jigai]]''), along with Katsuyori in the {{ill|Battle of Tenmokuzan|en|Battle of Tenmokuzan|WD=Q1113509|lt=Battle of Tenmokuzan}}. Both of his sons died in the battle. ==Family== Father: {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Takeda Shingen}} (1521–1573) Sons: * [[Takeda Nobukatsu]] (1567–1582) * [[Takeda Katsuchika]] (1580–1582) Wives: * [[Toyama Fujin]] * Keirin'in Daughters: * Tei-hime, married Miyahara Yoshihisa * Kougu-hime, married Naitō Tadaoki == References == {{Commons category-inline|Takeda Katsuyori}} {{Reflist}} == Further reading == * {{cite book | title=Japanese women poets: an anthology |author=Hiroaki Sato | year=2008 | publisher=M.E. Sharpe, Inc }} *[http://www.interq.or.jp/pure/miyatate/takeda-katsuyori.htm Takeda Katsuyori no Saiki (in Japanese)] *[https://web.archive.org/web/20070614061236/http://www.city.kofu.yamanashi.jp/kanko/history/0103.htm Yamanashi Prefecture page on Takeda Katsuyori (in Japanese)] *Shibatsuji Shunroku 柴辻俊六 and Hirayama Masaru 平山優. ''Takeda Katsuyori no Subete'' 武田勝頼のすべて. Tokyo: Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha 新人物往来社, 2007. *Shibatsuji Shunroku 柴辻俊六, ''Takeda Katsuyori'' 武田勝頼. Tokyo: Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha 新人物往来社, 2003. {{openhistory}} {{People of the Sengoku period|state=autocollapse}} ]] {{Suwa Faith}} [[file:Katsuyori died at Mt.Tenmoku 02.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Ukiyoe|Ukiyoe]] [[en:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[cs:Kacujori Takeda]] [[de:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[eo:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[es:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[fa:تاکدا کاتسویوری]] [[fr:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[id:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[it:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[ja:武田勝頼]] [[ko:다케다 가쓰요리]] [[ms:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[nl:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[pl:Katsuyori Takeda]] [[pt:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[ro:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[ru:Такэда Кацуёри]] [[sr:Такеда Кацујори]] [[th:ทาเกดะ คัตสึโยริ]] [[tr:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[uk:Такеда Кацуйорі]] [[zh:武田勝賴]] [[zh-min-nan:Takeda Katuyori]] {{wikidata link|Q662482}} [[Category:1582 deaths]] [[Category:Daimyo]] [[Category:Suicides by seppuku]] [[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:Takeda clan]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

[[File:Takeda mon.svg|20px]] [[Takeda clan]] | unit = | Rank = [[Daimyo]] | commands = {{ill|Shinpu Castle|en|Shinpu Castle|WD=Q11502234|lt=Shinpu Castle}} | battles = {{Plainlist| * {{ill|Siege of Kanbara|en|Siege of Kanbara|WD=Q2659520|lt=Siege of Kanbara}} * {{ill|Siege of Futamata|en|Siege of Futamata|WD=Q1014528|lt=Siege of Futamata}} * {{ill|Battle of Mikatagahara|en|Battle of Mikatagahara|WD=Q690464|lt=Battle of Mikatagahara}} * {{ill|Siege of Takatenjin (1574)|en|Siege of Takatenjin (1574)|WD=Q4540826|lt=Siege of Takatenjin}} * {{ill|Siege of Yoshida Castle|en|Siege of Yoshida Castle|WD=Q5055597|lt=Siege of Yoshida}} * {{ill|Battle of Nagashino|en|Battle of Nagashino|WD=Q428369|lt=Battle of Nagashino}} * {{ill|Battle of Omosu|en|Battle of Omosu|WD=Q610329|lt=Battle of Omosu}} * {{ill|Battle of Tenmokuzan|en|Battle of Tenmokuzan|WD=Q1113509|lt=Battle of Tenmokuzan}}{{KIA}} }} }} {{nihongo|'''Takeda Katsuyori'''|武田 勝頼||extra=1546 – 3 April 1582}} was a Japanese ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' (military lord) of the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, who was famed as the head of the [[Takeda clan]] and the successor to the legendary warlord {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Takeda Shingen}}. He was son-in-law of [[:en:Hojo Ujiyasu|Hojo Ujiyasu]], ''daimyō'' of Hojo clan. ==Early life== He was the son of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Shingen}} by the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige (daimyo)|en|Suwa Yorishige (daimyo)|WD=Q2511179|lt=Suwa Yorishige}} ({{ill|Posthumous name|en|Posthumous name|WD=Q696100|lt=Posthumous name}}:{{nihongo|''[[Suwa-goryōnin]]''|諏訪御料人||extra=real name, Koihime}}).{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=978-1-59020-730-7 |page=209 |author-link=Hiroaki Sato (translator)}} Katsuyori's children included Takeda Nobukatsu and Katsuchika.{{Cite web|url=http://www2.harimaya.com/sengoku/html/takeda.html|title=兵庫県西部、播磨地方の情報を中心に日本の旅の想い出を発信しています|language=Japanese|website=www2.harimaya.com}} Katsuyori, first known as {{nihongo|'''Suwa Shirō Katsuyori'''|諏訪四郎勝頼}}, succeeded to his mother's [[Suwa clan]] and gained Takatō Castle as the seat of his domain. After his elder brother {{ill|Takeda Yoshinobu|en|Takeda Yoshinobu|WD=Q837273|lt=Takeda Yoshinobu}} died, Katsuyori's son Nobukatsu became heir to the Takeda clan, making Katsuyori the ''de facto'' ruler of the Takeda clan.{{cite book |last1=Turnbull |first1=Stephen |title=Battles of the Samurai |date=1987 |publisher=Arms and Armour Press |isbn=978-0-85368-826-6 |location=London |pages=79–94}} Takeda Katsuyori built {{ill|Shinpu Castle|en|Shinpu Castle|WD=Q11502234|lt=Shinpu Castle}}, a new and larger castle at Nirasaki and transferred his residence there in 1581. ==Military life== In 1569, Katsuyori defeated [[Hojo Ujinobu]] at {{ill|Siege of Kanbara|en|Siege of Kanbara|WD=Q2659520|lt=Siege of Kanbara}}{{cite web|url=https://www.jalan.net/kankou/spt_22382af2170020709/ |title=蒲原城|publisher=じゃらん|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1572, Katsuyori successfully took a [[Tokugawa clan]] possession in the {{ill|Siege of Futamata|en|Siege of Futamata|WD=Q1014528|lt=Siege of Futamata}}, and participated in the {{ill|Battle of Mikatagahara|en|Battle of Mikatagahara|WD=Q690464|lt=Battle of Mikatagahara}} against the Oda-Tokugawa alliance.{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1-85409-523-4 |page=219,222–223,230}} In 1573, Katsuyori took charge of the Takeda family after the death of Shingen and fought the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In 1574, he {{ill|Siege of Takatenjin (1574)|en|Siege of Takatenjin (1574)|WD=Q4540826|lt=captured Takatenjin castle}}, which even his father had not managed to do. This gained him the support of other members of the Takeda clan. In 1575, he suffered a terrible loss at the {{ill|Battle of Nagashino|en|Battle of Nagashino|WD=Q428369|lt=Battle of Nagashino}}, defeated by one of the earliest recorded uses of [[:en:Volley fire (infantry tactic)|volley fire]] (by {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}'s 3,000 muskets), and losing a large part of his forces as well as a number of Takeda's generals and retainer.{{cite book |last1=Turnbull |first1=Stephen |title=The Samurai |date=1977 |publisher=Macmillan Publishing Co., Inc. |isbn=978-0-02-620540-5 |location=New York |pages=156–160}} In 1578, Katsuyori incurred the wrath of the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Hōjō family}} by helping {{ill|Uesugi Kagekatsu|en|Uesugi Kagekatsu|WD=Q1376605|lt=Uesugi Kagekatsu}} against {{ill|Uesugi Kagetora|en|Uesugi Kagetora|WD=Q1190934|lt=Uesugi Kagetora}} who was {{ill|Hōjō Ujiyasu|en|Hōjō Ujiyasu|WD=Q943643|lt=Hōjō Ujiyasu}}'s seventh son, adopted by and heir to {{ill|Uesugi Kenshin|en|Uesugi Kenshin|WD=Q311080|lt=Uesugi Kenshin}}, that initiated the {{ill|Battle of Omosu|en|Battle of Omosu|WD=Q610329|lt=Battle of Omosu}} in 1580 against [[:en:Hojo Ujimasa|Hojo Ujimasa]]. In 1581, Katsuyori {{ill|Siege of Takatenjin (1581)|en|Siege of Takatenjin (1581)|WD=Q2275430|lt=lost Takatenjin fortress}} by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, the siege ended with the deaths of 680 men of {{ill|Okabe Motonobu|en|Okabe Motonobu|WD=Q1073455|lt=Okabe Motonobu}} garrison.{{cite book |last1=Turnbull |first1=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |date=2000 |publisher=Cassell & C0 |isbn=1-85409-523-4 |location=London |page=231}} In 1582, Katsuyori {{ill|Siege of Takatō (1582)|en|Siege of Takatō (1582)|WD=Q2901847|lt=lost Takatō castle}} by {{ill|Oda Nobutada|en|Oda Nobutada|WD=Q2446917|lt=Oda Nobutada}}, the only Takeda stronghold in {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano province}} to put up any resistance to Nobunaga's final invasion of Takeda domain, the castle was taken on March the 2nd 1582.{{Cite book |last=Ōta |first=Gyūichi |title=The chronicle of Lord Nobunaga |date=2011 |publisher=Brill |others=J. S. A. Elisonas, Jeroen Pieter Lamers |isbn=978-90-04-20456-0 |location=Leiden |pages=426–441 |oclc=743693801}} ==Death== {{Main|Battle of Tenmokuzan}} After Katsuyori {{ill|Siege of Takatenjin (1581)|en|Siege of Takatenjin (1581)|WD=Q2275430|lt=lost Takatenjin fortress}} and {{ill|Siege of Takatō (1582)|en|Siege of Takatō (1582)|WD=Q2901847|lt=Takatō castle}}, many clans like Kiso and Anayama withdrew their support for Takeda. The Oda-Tokugawa alliance advanced into {{ill|Kai Province|en|Kai Province|WD=Q858076|lt=Kai Province}}, and laid siege to {{ill|Shinpu Castle|en|Shinpu Castle|WD=Q11502234|lt=Shinpu Castle}}, Katsuyori was unable to hold the castle with his remaining 300-400 men, so he set fire to Shinpu Castle and fled into the Tenmoku mountain. Later, his forces were destroyed by the combined armies of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} and {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} at the {{ill|Battle of Tenmokuzan|en|Battle of Tenmokuzan|WD=Q1113509|lt=Battle of Tenmokuzan}}, after which Katsuyori, his wife, and his son committed ritual suicide, known as {{ill|Seppuku|en|Seppuku|WD=Q133462|lt=Seppuku}}.{{rp|231}} It was the end of [[Takeda clan]]. The nun {{ill|Rikei|en|Rikei|WD=Q3432034|lt=Rikei}} wrote an account of his wife's suicide and, pitying them, wrote several verses in their honour.{{cite book |last1=Sato |first1=Hiroaki |title=Japanese Women Poets |date=2008 |publisher=M.E. Sharpe, Inc. |isbn=978-0-7656-1784-2 |location=Armonk |pages=137–139}} of Takeda Katsuyori at Tenmokuzan|alt=|center|426x426px]] == Spouse == [[File:Hojo Masako (Takeda Katsuyori's wife).jpg|thumb|Keirin'in (Katsuyori's wife) carrying a {{ill|Naginata|en|Naginata|WD=Q546116|lt=Naginata}} in the {{ill|Battle of Tenmokuzan|en|Battle of Tenmokuzan|WD=Q1113509|lt=Battle of Tenmokuzan}}.]] === Toyama Fujin === Takeda Katsuyori married Toyoma Fujin, the adopted daughter of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}. She died while giving birth to their son Nobukatsu in 1567. === Keirin'in === Katsuyori later married Keirin'in, daughter of {{ill|Hōjō Ujiyasu|en|Hōjō Ujiyasu|WD=Q943643|lt=Hojo Ujiyasu}}. They had a son and two daughters. In 1582, when Keirin'in was 19, Katsuyori was decisively defeated by Oda Nobunaga, and they had to flee. However, Katsuyori was resigned to die and urged her to leave him. She refused and killed herself (''[[:en:Jigai|Jigai]]''), along with Katsuyori in the {{ill|Battle of Tenmokuzan|en|Battle of Tenmokuzan|WD=Q1113509|lt=Battle of Tenmokuzan}}. Both of his sons died in the battle. ==Family== Father: {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Takeda Shingen}} (1521–1573) Sons: * [[Takeda Nobukatsu]] (1567–1582) * [[Takeda Katsuchika]] (1580–1582) Wives: * [[Toyama Fujin]] * Keirin'in Daughters: * Tei-hime, married Miyahara Yoshihisa * Kougu-hime, married Naitō Tadaoki == References == {{Commons category-inline|Takeda Katsuyori}} {{Reflist}} == Further reading == * {{cite book | title=Japanese women poets: an anthology |author=Hiroaki Sato | year=2008 | publisher=M.E. Sharpe, Inc }} *[http://www.interq.or.jp/pure/miyatate/takeda-katsuyori.htm Takeda Katsuyori no Saiki (in Japanese)] *[https://web.archive.org/web/20070614061236/http://www.city.kofu.yamanashi.jp/kanko/history/0103.htm Yamanashi Prefecture page on Takeda Katsuyori (in Japanese)] *Shibatsuji Shunroku 柴辻俊六 and Hirayama Masaru 平山優. ''Takeda Katsuyori no Subete'' 武田勝頼のすべて. Tokyo: Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha 新人物往来社, 2007. *Shibatsuji Shunroku 柴辻俊六, ''Takeda Katsuyori'' 武田勝頼. Tokyo: Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha 新人物往来社, 2003. {{openhistory}} {{People of the Sengoku period|state=autocollapse}} ]] {{Suwa Faith}} [[file:Katsuyori died at Mt.Tenmoku 02.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Ukiyoe|Ukiyoe]] [[en:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[cs:Kacujori Takeda]] [[de:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[eo:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[es:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[fa:تاکدا کاتسویوری]] [[fr:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[id:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[it:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[ja:武田勝頼]] [[ko:다케다 가쓰요리]] [[ms:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[nl:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[pl:Katsuyori Takeda]] [[pt:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[ro:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[ru:Такэда Кацуёри]] [[sr:Такеда Кацујори]] [[th:ทาเกดะ คัตสึโยริ]] [[tr:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[uk:Такеда Кацуйорі]] [[zh:武田勝賴]] [[zh-min-nan:Takeda Katuyori]] {{wikidata link|Q662482}} [[Category:1582 deaths]] [[Category:Daimyo]] [[Category:Suicides by seppuku]] [[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:Takeda clan]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

Kougu-hime | relatives = {{ill|Takeda Yoshinobu|en|Takeda Yoshinobu|WD=Q837273|lt=Takeda Yoshinobu}} (brother)
{{ill|Takeda Nobuchika|en|Takeda Nobuchika|WD=Q6582131|lt=Takeda Nobuchika}} (brother)
{{ill|Nishina Morinobu|en|Nishina Morinobu|WD=Q4168137|lt=Nishina Morinobu}} (brother)
{{ill|Hōjō Ujiyasu|en|Hōjō Ujiyasu|WD=Q943643|lt=Hōjō Ujiyasu}} (father-in-law) | monarch = | birth_date = 1546 | birth_place = {{ill|Kai Province|en|Kai Province|WD=Q858076|lt=Kai Province}} | death_date = {{death date and age|df=yes|1582|4|3|1546}} | death_place = Tenmoku Mountain, {{ill|Kai Province|en|Kai Province|WD=Q858076|lt=Kai Province}} | allegiance = [[Image:Japanese crest Suwa Kajinoha(Black background).svg|20px]] [[Suwa clan]]
[[File:Takeda mon.svg|20px]] [[Takeda clan]] | unit = | Rank = [[Daimyo]] | commands = {{ill|Shinpu Castle|en|Shinpu Castle|WD=Q11502234|lt=Shinpu Castle}} | battles = {{Plainlist| * {{ill|Siege of Kanbara|en|Siege of Kanbara|WD=Q2659520|lt=Siege of Kanbara}} * {{ill|Siege of Futamata|en|Siege of Futamata|WD=Q1014528|lt=Siege of Futamata}} * {{ill|Battle of Mikatagahara|en|Battle of Mikatagahara|WD=Q690464|lt=Battle of Mikatagahara}} * {{ill|Siege of Takatenjin (1574)|en|Siege of Takatenjin (1574)|WD=Q4540826|lt=Siege of Takatenjin}} * {{ill|Siege of Yoshida Castle|en|Siege of Yoshida Castle|WD=Q5055597|lt=Siege of Yoshida}} * {{ill|Battle of Nagashino|en|Battle of Nagashino|WD=Q428369|lt=Battle of Nagashino}} * {{ill|Battle of Omosu|en|Battle of Omosu|WD=Q610329|lt=Battle of Omosu}} * {{ill|Battle of Tenmokuzan|en|Battle of Tenmokuzan|WD=Q1113509|lt=Battle of Tenmokuzan}}{{KIA}} }} }} {{nihongo|'''Takeda Katsuyori'''|武田 勝頼||extra=1546 – 3 April 1582}} was a Japanese ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' (military lord) of the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, who was famed as the head of the [[Takeda clan]] and the successor to the legendary warlord {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Takeda Shingen}}. He was son-in-law of [[:en:Hojo Ujiyasu|Hojo Ujiyasu]], ''daimyō'' of Hojo clan. ==Early life== He was the son of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Shingen}} by the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige (daimyo)|en|Suwa Yorishige (daimyo)|WD=Q2511179|lt=Suwa Yorishige}} ({{ill|Posthumous name|en|Posthumous name|WD=Q696100|lt=Posthumous name}}:{{nihongo|''[[Suwa-goryōnin]]''|諏訪御料人||extra=real name, Koihime}}).{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=978-1-59020-730-7 |page=209 |author-link=Hiroaki Sato (translator)}} Katsuyori's children included Takeda Nobukatsu and Katsuchika.{{Cite web|url=http://www2.harimaya.com/sengoku/html/takeda.html|title=兵庫県西部、播磨地方の情報を中心に日本の旅の想い出を発信しています|language=Japanese|website=www2.harimaya.com}} Katsuyori, first known as {{nihongo|'''Suwa Shirō Katsuyori'''|諏訪四郎勝頼}}, succeeded to his mother's [[Suwa clan]] and gained Takatō Castle as the seat of his domain. After his elder brother {{ill|Takeda Yoshinobu|en|Takeda Yoshinobu|WD=Q837273|lt=Takeda Yoshinobu}} died, Katsuyori's son Nobukatsu became heir to the Takeda clan, making Katsuyori the ''de facto'' ruler of the Takeda clan.{{cite book |last1=Turnbull |first1=Stephen |title=Battles of the Samurai |date=1987 |publisher=Arms and Armour Press |isbn=978-0-85368-826-6 |location=London |pages=79–94}} Takeda Katsuyori built {{ill|Shinpu Castle|en|Shinpu Castle|WD=Q11502234|lt=Shinpu Castle}}, a new and larger castle at Nirasaki and transferred his residence there in 1581. ==Military life== In 1569, Katsuyori defeated [[Hojo Ujinobu]] at {{ill|Siege of Kanbara|en|Siege of Kanbara|WD=Q2659520|lt=Siege of Kanbara}}{{cite web|url=https://www.jalan.net/kankou/spt_22382af2170020709/ |title=蒲原城|publisher=じゃらん|language=ja|access-date=25 July 2019}} In 1572, Katsuyori successfully took a [[Tokugawa clan]] possession in the {{ill|Siege of Futamata|en|Siege of Futamata|WD=Q1014528|lt=Siege of Futamata}}, and participated in the {{ill|Battle of Mikatagahara|en|Battle of Mikatagahara|WD=Q690464|lt=Battle of Mikatagahara}} against the Oda-Tokugawa alliance.{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1-85409-523-4 |page=219,222–223,230}} In 1573, Katsuyori took charge of the Takeda family after the death of Shingen and fought the [[Tokugawa clan]]. In 1574, he {{ill|Siege of Takatenjin (1574)|en|Siege of Takatenjin (1574)|WD=Q4540826|lt=captured Takatenjin castle}}, which even his father had not managed to do. This gained him the support of other members of the Takeda clan. In 1575, he suffered a terrible loss at the {{ill|Battle of Nagashino|en|Battle of Nagashino|WD=Q428369|lt=Battle of Nagashino}}, defeated by one of the earliest recorded uses of [[:en:Volley fire (infantry tactic)|volley fire]] (by {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}'s 3,000 muskets), and losing a large part of his forces as well as a number of Takeda's generals and retainer.{{cite book |last1=Turnbull |first1=Stephen |title=The Samurai |date=1977 |publisher=Macmillan Publishing Co., Inc. |isbn=978-0-02-620540-5 |location=New York |pages=156–160}} In 1578, Katsuyori incurred the wrath of the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Hōjō family}} by helping {{ill|Uesugi Kagekatsu|en|Uesugi Kagekatsu|WD=Q1376605|lt=Uesugi Kagekatsu}} against {{ill|Uesugi Kagetora|en|Uesugi Kagetora|WD=Q1190934|lt=Uesugi Kagetora}} who was {{ill|Hōjō Ujiyasu|en|Hōjō Ujiyasu|WD=Q943643|lt=Hōjō Ujiyasu}}'s seventh son, adopted by and heir to {{ill|Uesugi Kenshin|en|Uesugi Kenshin|WD=Q311080|lt=Uesugi Kenshin}}, that initiated the {{ill|Battle of Omosu|en|Battle of Omosu|WD=Q610329|lt=Battle of Omosu}} in 1580 against [[:en:Hojo Ujimasa|Hojo Ujimasa]]. In 1581, Katsuyori {{ill|Siege of Takatenjin (1581)|en|Siege of Takatenjin (1581)|WD=Q2275430|lt=lost Takatenjin fortress}} by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, the siege ended with the deaths of 680 men of {{ill|Okabe Motonobu|en|Okabe Motonobu|WD=Q1073455|lt=Okabe Motonobu}} garrison.{{cite book |last1=Turnbull |first1=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |date=2000 |publisher=Cassell & C0 |isbn=1-85409-523-4 |location=London |page=231}} In 1582, Katsuyori {{ill|Siege of Takatō (1582)|en|Siege of Takatō (1582)|WD=Q2901847|lt=lost Takatō castle}} by {{ill|Oda Nobutada|en|Oda Nobutada|WD=Q2446917|lt=Oda Nobutada}}, the only Takeda stronghold in {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano province}} to put up any resistance to Nobunaga's final invasion of Takeda domain, the castle was taken on March the 2nd 1582.{{Cite book |last=Ōta |first=Gyūichi |title=The chronicle of Lord Nobunaga |date=2011 |publisher=Brill |others=J. S. A. Elisonas, Jeroen Pieter Lamers |isbn=978-90-04-20456-0 |location=Leiden |pages=426–441 |oclc=743693801}} ==Death== {{Main|Battle of Tenmokuzan}} After Katsuyori {{ill|Siege of Takatenjin (1581)|en|Siege of Takatenjin (1581)|WD=Q2275430|lt=lost Takatenjin fortress}} and {{ill|Siege of Takatō (1582)|en|Siege of Takatō (1582)|WD=Q2901847|lt=Takatō castle}}, many clans like Kiso and Anayama withdrew their support for Takeda. The Oda-Tokugawa alliance advanced into {{ill|Kai Province|en|Kai Province|WD=Q858076|lt=Kai Province}}, and laid siege to {{ill|Shinpu Castle|en|Shinpu Castle|WD=Q11502234|lt=Shinpu Castle}}, Katsuyori was unable to hold the castle with his remaining 300-400 men, so he set fire to Shinpu Castle and fled into the Tenmoku mountain. Later, his forces were destroyed by the combined armies of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}} and {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} at the {{ill|Battle of Tenmokuzan|en|Battle of Tenmokuzan|WD=Q1113509|lt=Battle of Tenmokuzan}}, after which Katsuyori, his wife, and his son committed ritual suicide, known as {{ill|Seppuku|en|Seppuku|WD=Q133462|lt=Seppuku}}.{{rp|231}} It was the end of [[Takeda clan]]. The nun {{ill|Rikei|en|Rikei|WD=Q3432034|lt=Rikei}} wrote an account of his wife's suicide and, pitying them, wrote several verses in their honour.{{cite book |last1=Sato |first1=Hiroaki |title=Japanese Women Poets |date=2008 |publisher=M.E. Sharpe, Inc. |isbn=978-0-7656-1784-2 |location=Armonk |pages=137–139}} of Takeda Katsuyori at Tenmokuzan|alt=|center|426x426px]] == Spouse == [[File:Hojo Masako (Takeda Katsuyori's wife).jpg|thumb|Keirin'in (Katsuyori's wife) carrying a {{ill|Naginata|en|Naginata|WD=Q546116|lt=Naginata}} in the {{ill|Battle of Tenmokuzan|en|Battle of Tenmokuzan|WD=Q1113509|lt=Battle of Tenmokuzan}}.]] === Toyama Fujin === Takeda Katsuyori married Toyoma Fujin, the adopted daughter of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}. She died while giving birth to their son Nobukatsu in 1567. === Keirin'in === Katsuyori later married Keirin'in, daughter of {{ill|Hōjō Ujiyasu|en|Hōjō Ujiyasu|WD=Q943643|lt=Hojo Ujiyasu}}. They had a son and two daughters. In 1582, when Keirin'in was 19, Katsuyori was decisively defeated by Oda Nobunaga, and they had to flee. However, Katsuyori was resigned to die and urged her to leave him. She refused and killed herself (''[[:en:Jigai|Jigai]]''), along with Katsuyori in the {{ill|Battle of Tenmokuzan|en|Battle of Tenmokuzan|WD=Q1113509|lt=Battle of Tenmokuzan}}. Both of his sons died in the battle. ==Family== Father: {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Takeda Shingen}} (1521–1573) Sons: * [[Takeda Nobukatsu]] (1567–1582) * [[Takeda Katsuchika]] (1580–1582) Wives: * [[Toyama Fujin]] * Keirin'in Daughters: * Tei-hime, married Miyahara Yoshihisa * Kougu-hime, married Naitō Tadaoki == References == {{Commons category-inline|Takeda Katsuyori}} {{Reflist}} == Further reading == * {{cite book | title=Japanese women poets: an anthology |author=Hiroaki Sato | year=2008 | publisher=M.E. Sharpe, Inc }} *[http://www.interq.or.jp/pure/miyatate/takeda-katsuyori.htm Takeda Katsuyori no Saiki (in Japanese)] *[https://web.archive.org/web/20070614061236/http://www.city.kofu.yamanashi.jp/kanko/history/0103.htm Yamanashi Prefecture page on Takeda Katsuyori (in Japanese)] *Shibatsuji Shunroku 柴辻俊六 and Hirayama Masaru 平山優. ''Takeda Katsuyori no Subete'' 武田勝頼のすべて. Tokyo: Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha 新人物往来社, 2007. *Shibatsuji Shunroku 柴辻俊六, ''Takeda Katsuyori'' 武田勝頼. Tokyo: Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha 新人物往来社, 2003. {{openhistory}} {{People of the Sengoku period|state=autocollapse}} ]] {{Suwa Faith}} [[file:Katsuyori died at Mt.Tenmoku 02.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Ukiyoe|Ukiyoe]] [[en:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[cs:Kacujori Takeda]] [[de:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[eo:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[es:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[fa:تاکدا کاتسویوری]] [[fr:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[id:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[it:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[ja:武田勝頼]] [[ko:다케다 가쓰요리]] [[ms:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[nl:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[pl:Katsuyori Takeda]] [[pt:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[ro:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[ru:Такэда Кацуёри]] [[sr:Такеда Кацујори]] [[th:ทาเกดะ คัตสึโยริ]] [[tr:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[uk:Такеда Кацуйорі]] [[zh:武田勝賴]] [[zh-min-nan:Takeda Katuyori]] {{wikidata link|Q662482}} [[Category:1582 deaths]] [[Category:Daimyo]] [[Category:Suicides by seppuku]] [[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:Takeda clan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]
